skill.name: pytorch-lightning
skill.description: Guides PyTorch Lightning project structure, including LightningModules, DataModules, Trainers, callbacks, logging, and distributed training patterns.
skill.summary: Build scalable PyTorch Lightning training workflows with structured model, data, trainer, logging, and distributed training guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Manual review found no prompt injection, hidden command execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior. Most static command and weak-crypto findings are false positives from Markdown backticks and PyTorch Lightning terminology. Remaining risks are low-risk documentation examples and local Python templates that users may choose to run.

low_findings.title: Static Command Matches Are Documentation-Only
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution. Reviewed examples are method names, configuration snippets, or documentation command examples, not hidden executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sampled static command hits are inside Markdown prose or fenced examples. No executable shell wrapper or command-dispatch code was found in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Executable Python Templates Are Present
low_findings.description: The skill includes Python template files that users may run or copy. They import PyTorch Lightning libraries and define trainer, module, and data module examples without subprocess or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts contain static imports and training boilerplate. The reviewed content does not show dynamic imports, eval on user input, subprocess usage, or network calls.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network And Tracking Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation includes example URLs, localhost tracking, and private multi-node training addresses. These are instructional placeholders and do not show data exfiltration or unauthorized outbound calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network values are example.com, localhost, and a private LAN address in documentation. No code path sends secrets or user data to those addresses.

low_findings.title: Placeholder API Key Parameters In Logger Examples
low_findings.description: The logging guide shows API key parameters using literal placeholder text. No real credential is embedded, but users should source real keys from secure configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only credential-like values are literal YOUR_API_KEY placeholders in documentation examples. This is not evidence of credential harvesting or leaked secrets.

low_findings.title: Local Environment Variable Example
low_findings.description: The distributed training guide sets NCCL_TIMEOUT for cluster training. This is a local runtime configuration example, not sensitive environment access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable name and value are non-secret distributed-training settings. No environment secrets are read, logged, or transmitted.

content.value_statement: PyTorch training code can become hard to scale and reproduce. This skill guides LightningModules, DataModules, Trainers, callbacks, logging, and distributed training patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to structure PyTorch models as LightningModules.
Provides trainer configuration guidance for debugging, production, and multi-GPU training.
Shows DataModule patterns for reusable data preparation and dataloaders.
Covers callbacks for checkpointing, early stopping, monitoring, and custom hooks.
Documents logger setup for TensorBoard, W&B, MLflow, Comet, Neptune, and CSV logging.
Compares DDP, FSDP, and DeepSpeed strategies for distributed training.
limitations: Does not train models or run experiments by itself.
Examples require PyTorch Lightning and related Python packages in the user environment.
Distributed training guidance still depends on cluster, GPU, and network configuration.
Template code must be adapted to the user dataset, model architecture, and metrics.

use_cases.title: Convert PyTorch Code To Lightning
use_cases.description: Refactor a manual training loop into LightningModule methods with clear optimizer and logging structure.

use_cases.title: Create Reusable Data Pipelines
use_cases.description: Organize dataset preparation, splits, transforms, and dataloaders inside a LightningDataModule.

use_cases.title: Plan Distributed Training Runs
use_cases.description: Choose DDP, FSDP, or DeepSpeed settings for larger models and multi-GPU training jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Start A LightningModule
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a PyTorch LightningModule for my model. Ask for my input shape, output target, loss, optimizer, and metrics first.

prompt_templates.title: Build A DataModule
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a LightningDataModule plan for my dataset. Include prepare_data, setup, dataloaders, transforms, and train validation split choices.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my training requirements and recommend Trainer settings, callbacks, checkpointing, logging, precision, and debugging options.

prompt_templates.title: Scale Distributed Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare DDP, FSDP, and DeepSpeed for my model size and hardware. Recommend a Lightning strategy and explain operational tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to organize an image classifier in PyTorch Lightning.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with LightningModule responsibilities, training and validation steps, optimizer selection, metric logging, and checkpoint recommendations.

output_examples.input: A user asks why validation metrics differ across multi-GPU runs.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering distributed metric synchronization, dataloader shuffling, validation device count, batch sizes, and logging settings.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether to use DDP, FSDP, or DeepSpeed.
output_examples.output: A strategy comparison based on model size, GPU memory, cluster complexity, checkpoint needs, and expected training scale.

best_practices: Start with fast_dev_run and CPU debugging before scaling to GPU or distributed runs.
Keep model logic in LightningModule and reusable data logic in LightningDataModule.
Use callbacks and loggers for monitoring, checkpointing, early stopping, and experiment tracking.
anti_patterns: Do not place dataset state in prepare_data because it does not transfer across distributed processes.
Do not hardcode real tracking API keys in training scripts or notebooks.
Do not choose distributed strategies before confirming model size, memory needs, and cluster constraints.

faq.question: Does this skill include runnable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes local Python templates for LightningModules, DataModules, and Trainer configurations.

faq.question: Can it replace PyTorch Lightning documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes common patterns and examples, but users should verify version-specific API details.

faq.question: Does it support distributed training?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, multi-node setup, and common distributed training settings.

faq.question: Does it manage experiment tracking credentials?
faq.answer: No. It shows logger parameters, but users must manage real credentials securely outside the examples.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users customize first?
faq.answer: Users should customize the dataset, model architecture, loss function, metrics, optimizer, and training hardware settings.
